did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses machine learning models (SVM, Random Forest, and Neural Networks) trained on the ISIC dataset to perform skin cancer detection. The study investigates the effectiveness of adversarial training and data poisoning attacks against these AI models in the context of dermatology.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results investigating data poisoning attacks against adversarially trained machine learning models (SVM, Random Forest, Neural Networks) using the ISIC dataset. It includes specific methodology sections (Experimental Setup, Adversarial Training, Model Poisoning), quantitative results (accuracy, misclassification rates, model variance), and new findings regarding the effectiveness of adversarial training on baseline ML models, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Exploring Data Poisoning Attacks Against Adversarially Trained Skin Cancer Diagnostics
journal: 2024 IEEE/ACM 17th International Conference on Utility and Cloud Computing (UCC)
brief_summary: This study investigates the effectiveness of adversarial training in protecting traditional machine learning models, specifically SVM, Random Forest, and Neural Networks, against data poisoning attacks in skin cancer diagnostics. Using the ProveAI version of the ISIC dataset with four classes, the authors implemented a 10% outlier data poisoning attack leveraging the Fast Gradient Sign Method (FGSM). The evaluation focused on classification accuracy, model variance, and confidence score disruptions in randomly sampled predictions. Results indicated that while adversarial training successfully secured neural networks with only a 2% accuracy degradation, it failed to protect SVM and Random Forest models, which suffered accuracy drops to 51.2% and 27.5%, respectively. The study concludes that adversarial training is not universally effective for baseline ML models, as SVM exhibited a 100% change in individual sample diagnosis confidence under attack.

lesion_types_studied.details: Largest class in the test dataset with 115 samples. Used as a primary label for misclassification rate analysis and confidence score disruption evaluation.
Smallest class in the test dataset with 5 samples. Used as a primary label for misclassification rate analysis.
Intermediate class with 7 samples. Used in misclassification rate analysis.
Intermediate class with 24 samples. Used in misclassification rate analysis.

all_task_types.target: Skin cancer diagnostic categories (Benign, Malignant, Indeterminate/Benign, Indeterminate/Malignant)
all_task_types.details: The paper investigates multi-class classification of skin lesions using the ISIC dataset with four classes. The models (SVM, Random Forest, Neural Networks) predict discrete diagnostic labels. Evaluation metrics include accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and false positive rate, which are standard for classification tasks. The study also analyzes misclassification rates and confidence scores for class prediction.

datasets_used.name: ProveAI version of the ISIC dataset
datasets_used.description: A public skin cancer image dataset used for multi-class skin cancer diagnosis. The study utilized the ProveAI version specifically, which contains four classes of skin lesions.

use.training: Used for training baseline ML models (SVM, RF, NN) and for adversarial training (75% of the dataset). A subset (80-20% distribution) was used by the adversary for poisoning the training set.
use.testing: Used for testing the performance of cleaned and poisoned models (151 instances total).
use.other: Used to generate poisoned samples via outlier data poisoning attack with a 10% poisoning level.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To assess the sensitivity and integrity of the models (SVM, RF, NN) against data poisoning attacks by measuring the change in prediction variance.
To evaluate the impact of data poisoning on individual sample diagnoses by analyzing changes in prediction confidence scores via random sampling.
To quantify the performance degradation of models on specific dataset classes (Benign, Malignant, Indeterminate/Benign, Indeterminate/Malignant) under poisoned conditions.

statistical_methods_used.method: Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-score, and False Positive Rate (FPR) Comparison
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To compare the classification performance of cleaned versus poisoned adversarially trained models (SVM, RF, NN).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. It reported descriptive statistics such as accuracy, misclassification rates, model variance, and confidence score changes.

evaluation_settings.description: Models trained on clean data without adversarial training or poisoning
Models trained with adversarial training (75% clean, 25% poisoned) and evaluated on poisoned test set (80/20 split of poisoned training data)

poisoned_metrics.confidence_disruption: 100% change in individual sample diagnosis confidence

poisoned_metrics.accuracy_degradation: Decreased to 27.5% (Note: Text states 27.5% in abstract, Table III shows 58.2% for poisoned RF. Abstract likely refers to a different metric or specific subset, but Table III is the detailed result. The abstract explicitly states 'accuracy decreased to ... 27.5%' for Random Forest. Table III shows 58.2. I will include both as reported in different sections).

poisoned_metrics.confidence_disruption: Minor changes; e.g., 0.1/0.86 confidence for Benign sample; 0.2/0.55 shift from Benign to Indeterminate/Benign

ISIC_dataset.notes: Adversarial training successfully secured neural networks with only a 2% accuracy degradation. It failed to protect SVM and Random Forest models. SVM exhibited a 100% change in individual sample diagnosis confidence. The attack success rate against adversarially trained models was reported as 50% in the introduction.

architecture_used.main_model: Baseline Machine Learning models: Support Vector Machine (SVM), Random Forest (RF), and Neural Network (NN)
architecture_used.pretraining: None reported; models were trained from scratch using the ProveAI version of the ISIC dataset.
architecture_used.input_size: Not explicitly reported as fixed dimensions; data was converted to tensors (dtype=float32 for images, dtype=torch.long for labels).

Neural_Network.implementation: Implemented via PyTorch (torch.nn.Module) and Scikit-learn wrapper
Neural_Network.loss_function: Mean of clamped output (-1 to 1) plus L2 regularization term (0.01 * weight.t()@weight)/2.0

training_details.dataset: ProveAI version of the ISIC dataset (4 classes)
training_details.adversarial_training_method: Adversarial training using Fast Gradient Sign Method (FGSM) to inject poisoned samples into the training set
training_details.hardware: Linux Ubuntu machine on Dell PowerEdge server T640 with 4-core CPUs

preprocessing_techniques: Conversion of images to tensors with dtype=float32
Conversion of labels to tensors with dtype=torch.long
Application of Fast Gradient Sign Method (FGSM) for adversarial training and data poisoning attack generation
conclusions: Adversarial training is highly effective in securing Neural Networks against data poisoning attacks, with only a 2% degradation in accuracy.

Adversarial training failed to protect traditional baseline machine learning models, specifically Support Vector Machines (SVM) and Random Forest (RF), which suffered significant accuracy decreases to 51.2% and 27.5%, respectively, under a 10% data poisoning attack.
SVM is the most vulnerable model to data poisoning, exhibiting a 100% change in individual sample diagnosis confidence and the highest increase in model variance (from 0.9 to 1.15).
Random Forest and Neural Networks observed minor changes in their confidence scores compared to SVM, indicating greater stability in diagnosis behavior under attack.

The study demonstrates that adversarial training is not universally effective for all baseline ML models in non-complex settings, as it was ineffective for SVM and RF but significant for NN resilience.
The outlier data poisoning attack successfully evaded the adversarially trained models with an overall success rate of 50%.
important_remarks: The study relies on a very small dataset (151 total samples), with extreme class imbalance (e.g., only 5 Malignant samples), which limits the statistical power and generalizability of the findings.

Adversarial training was found to be ineffective for traditional baseline ML models (SVM and Random Forest), causing accuracy drops to 51.2% and 27.5% respectively, contradicting its common application in deep learning.
SVM exhibited the highest vulnerability, showing a 100% change in individual sample diagnosis confidence under attack, indicating a complete loss of diagnostic stability.
The attack success rate against adversarially trained models was reported as 50%, suggesting that the proposed defense mechanism is not robust against the specific outlier data poisoning attack used.

The research focuses on 'non-complex settings' using baseline ML models rather than complex deep learning architectures, which may not reflect the security landscape of modern, state-of-the-art skin cancer diagnostics.
The study does not report formal statistical significance tests (p-values), relying instead on descriptive metrics like accuracy, variance, and confidence score changes.
The experimental setup uses a specific 10% poisoning level based on prior literature [23], but does not explore the sensitivity of model resilience across varying poisoning intensities.
